text: The parables of Solomon, the son of David, king of Israel,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To know wisdom, and instruction:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To understand the words of prudence: and to receive the instruction of doctrine, justice, and judgment, and equity:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To give subtilty to little ones, to the young man knowledge and understanding.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wise man shall hear, and shall be wiser: and he that understandeth shall possess governments.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall understand a parable and the interpretation, the words of the wise, and their mysterious sayings.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom. Fools despise wisdom and instruction.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, hear the instruction of thy father, and forsake not the law of thy mother:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That grace may be added to thy head, and a chain of gold to thy neck.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, if sinners shall entice thee, consent not to them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If they shall say: Come with us, let us lie in wait for blood, let us hide snares for the innocent without cause:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let us swallow him up alive like hell, and whole as one that goeth down into the pit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We shall find all precious substance, we shall fill our houses with spoils.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Cast in thy lot with us, let us all have one purse.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, walk not thou with them, restrain thy foot from their paths.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For their feet run to evil, and make haste to shed blood.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But a net is spread in vain before the eyes of them that have wings.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they themselves lie in wait for their own blood, and practise deceits against their own souls.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So the ways of every covetous man destroy the souls of the possessors.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wisdom preacheth abroad, she uttereth her voice in the streets:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: At the head of multitudes she crieth out, in the entrance of the gates of the city she uttereth her words, saying:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O children, how long will you love childishness, and fools covet those things which are hurtful to themselves, and the unwise hate knowledge?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Turn ye at my reproof: behold I will utter my spirit to you, and will shew you my words.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because I called, and you refused: I stretched out my hand, and there was none that regarded.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: You have despised all my counsel, and have neglected my reprehensions.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I also will laugh in your destruction, and will mock when that shall come to you which you feared.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When sudden calamity shall fall on you, and destruction, as a tempest, shall be at hand: when tribulation and distress shall come upon you:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then shall they call upon me, and I will not hear: they shall rise in the morning, and shall not find me:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because they have hated instruction, and received not the fear of the Lord,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Nor consented to my counsel, but despised all my reproof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore they shall eat the fruit of their own way, and shall be filled with their own devices.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The turning away of little ones shall kill them, and the prosperity of fools shall destroy them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he that shall hear me, shall rest without terror, and shall enjoy abundance, without fear of evils.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, if thou wilt receive my words, and wilt hide my commandments with thee,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That thy ear may hearken to wisdom: incline thy heart to know prudence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For if thou shalt call for wisdom, and incline thy heart to prudence:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou shalt seek her as money, and shalt dig for her as for a treasure:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then shalt thou understand the fear of the Lord, and shalt find the knowledge of God:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because the Lord giveth wisdom: and out of his mouth cometh prudence and knowledge.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He wilt keep the salvation of the righteous, and protect them that walk in simplicity,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Keeping the paths of justice, and guarding the ways of saints.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then shalt thou understand justice, and judgment, and equity, and every good path.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If wisdom shall enter into thy heart, and knowledge please thy soul:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Counsel shall keep thee, and prudence shall preserve thee,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That thou mayst be delivered from the evil way, and from the man that speaketh perverse things:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who leave the right way, and walk by dark ways:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who are glad when they have done evil, and rejoice in the most wicked things:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whose ways are perverse, and their steps infamous.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That thou mayst be delivered from the strange woman, and from the stranger, who softeneth her words;

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And forsaketh the guide of her youth,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And hath forgotten the covenant of her God: for her house inclineth unto death, and her paths to hell.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: None that go in unto her, shall return again, neither shall they take hold of the paths of life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That thou mayst walk in a good way: and mayst keep the paths of the just.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For they that are upright, shall dwell in the earth; and the simple shall continue in it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the wicked shall be destroyed from the earth: and they that do unjustly, shall be taken away from it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, forget not my law, and let thy heart keep my commandments.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For they shall add to thee length of days, and years of life, and peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let not mercy and truth leave thee, put them about thy neck, and write them in the tables of thy heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thou shalt find grace, and good understanding before God and men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Have confidence in the Lord with all thy heart, and lean not upon thy own prudence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In all thy ways think on him, and he will direct thy steps.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not wise in thy own conceit: fear God, and depart from evil:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For it shall be health to thy navel, and moistening to thy bones.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Honour the Lord with thy substance, and give him of the first of all thy fruits;

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thy barns shall be filled with abundance, and thy presses shall run over with wine.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, reject not the correction of the Lord: and do not faint when thou art chastised by him:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For whom the Lord loveth, he chastiseth: and as a father in the son he pleaseth himself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Blessed is the man that findeth wisdom, and is rich in prudence:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The purchasing thereof is better than the merchandise of silver, and her fruit than the chief and purest gold:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She is more precious than all riches: and all the things that are desired, are not to be compared to her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Length of days is in her right hand, and in her left hand riches and glory.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Her ways are beautiful ways, and all her paths are peaceable.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She is a tree of life to them that lay hold on her: and he that shall retain her is blessed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord by wisdom hath founded the earth, hath established the heavens by prudence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By his wisdom the depths have broken out, and the clouds grow thick with dew.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, let not these things depart from thy eyes: keep the law and counsel:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there shall be life to thy soul, and grace to thy mouth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then shalt thou walk confidently in thy way, and thy foot shall not stumble:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou sleep, thou shalt not fear: thou shalt rest, and thy sleep shall be sweet.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not afraid of sudden fear, nor of the power of the wicked falling upon thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the Lord will be at thy side, and will keep thy foot that thou be not taken.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Do not withhold him from doing good, who is able: if thou art able, do good thyself also.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Say not to thy friend: Go, and come again: and to morrow I will give to thee: when thou canst give at present.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Practise not evil against thy friend, when he hath confidence in thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Strive not against a man without cause, when he hath done thee no evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Envy not the unjust man, and do not follow his ways.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For every mocker is an abomination to the Lord, and his communication is with the simple.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Want is from the Lord in the house of the wicked: but the habitations of the just shall be blessed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall scorn the scorners, and to the meek he will give grace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wise shall possess glory: the promotion of fools is disgrace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hear, ye children, the instruction of a father, and attend, that you may know prudence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I will give you a good gift, forsake not my law.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For I also was my father’s son, tender, and as an only son in the sight of my mother:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he taught me, and said: Let thy heart receive my words, keep my commandments, and thou shalt live.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Get wisdom, get prudence: forget not, neither decline from the words of my mouth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Forsake her not, and she shall keep thee: love her, and she shall preserve thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The beginning of wisdom, get wisdom, and with all thy possession purchase prudence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Take hold on her, and she shall exalt thee: thou shalt be glorified by her, when thou shalt embrace her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She shall give to thy head increase of graces, and protect thee with a noble crown.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hear, O my son, and receive my words, that years of life may be multiplied to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I will shew thee the way of wisdom, I will lead thee by the paths of equity:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Which when thou shalt have entered, thy steps shall not be straitened, and when thou runnest, thou shalt not meet a stumblingblock.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Take hold on instruction, leave it not: keep it, because it is thy life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not delighted in the paths of the wicked, neither let the way of evil men please thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Flee from it, pass not by it: go aside, and forsake it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For they sleep not, except they have done evil: and their sleep is taken away unless they have made some to fall.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They eat the bread of wickedness, and drink the wine of iniquity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the path of the just, as a shining light, goeth forwards, and increaseth even to perfect day.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The way of the wicked is darksome: they know not where they fall.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, hearken to my words, and incline thy ear to my sayings.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let them not depart from thy eyes, keep them in the midst of thy heart:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For they are life to those that find them, and health to all flesh.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With all watchfulness keep thy heart, because life issueth out from it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Remove from thee a froward mouth, and let detracting lips be far from thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let thy eyes look straight on, and let thy eyelids go before thy steps.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Make straight the path for thy feet, and all thy ways shall be established.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Decline not to the right hand, nor to the left: turn away thy foot from evil. For the Lord knoweth the ways that are on the right hand: but those are perverse which are on the left hand. But he will make thy courses straight, he will bring forward thy ways in peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, attend to my wisdom, and incline thy ear to my prudence,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That thou mayst keep thoughts, and thy lips may preserve instruction. Mind not the deceit of a woman.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the lips of a harlot are like a honeycomb dropping, and her throat is smoother than oil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But her end is bitter as wormwood, and sharp as a two-edged sword.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Her feet go down into death, and her steps go in as far as hell.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They walk not by the path of life, her steps are wandering, and unaccountable.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now, therefore, my son, hear me, and depart not from the words of my mouth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Remove thy way far from her, and come not nigh the doors of her house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give not thy honour to strangers, and thy years to the cruel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lest strangers be filled with thy strength, and thy labours be in another man’s house,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thou mourn at the last, when thou shalt have spent thy flesh and thy body, and say;

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Why have I hated instruction, and my heart consented not to reproof,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And have not heard the voice of them that taught me, and have not inclined my ear to masters?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have almost been in all evil, in the midst of the church and of the congregation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Drink water out of thy own cistern, and the streams of thy own well:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let thy fountains be conveyed abroad, and in the streets divide thy waters.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Keep them to thyself alone, neither let strangers be partakers with thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let thy vein be blessed, and rejoice with the wife of thy youth:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let her be thy dearest hind, and most agreeable fawn: let her breasts inebriate thee at all times: be thou delighted continually with her love.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Why art thou seduced, my son, by a strange woman, and art cherished in the bosom of another?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord beholdeth the ways of man, and considereth all his steps.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His own iniquities catch the wicked, and he is fast bound with the ropes of his own sins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall die, because he hath not received instruction, and in the multitude of his folly he shall be deceived.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, if thou be surety for thy friend, thou hast engaged fast thy hand to a stranger,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou art ensnared with the words of thy mouth, and caught with thy own words.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Do, therefore, my son, what I say, and deliver thyself: because thou art fallen into the hand of thy neighbour. Run about, make haste, stir up thy friend:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give not sleep to thy eyes, neither let thy eyelids slumber.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Deliver thyself as a doe from the hand, and as a bird from the hand of the fowler.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Go to the ant, O sluggard, and consider her ways, and learn wisdom:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Which, although she hath no guide, nor master, nor captain,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Provideth her meat for herself in the summer, and gathereth her food in the harvest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: How long wilt thou sleep, O sluggard? when wilt thou arise out of thy sleep?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou wilt sleep a little, thou wilt slumber a little, thou wilt fold thy hands a little to sleep:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And want shall come upon thee, as a traveller, and poverty as a man armed. But if thou be diligent, thy harvest shall come as a fountain, and want shall flee far from thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man that is an apostate, an unprofitable man, walketh with a perverse mouth,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He winketh with the eyes, presseth with the foot, speaketh with the finger.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With a wicked heart he deviseth evil, and at all times he soweth discord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To such a one his destruction shall presently come, and he shall suddenly be destroyed, and shall no longer have any remedy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Six things there are, which the Lord hateth, and the seventh his soul detesteth:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Haughty eyes, a lying tongue, hands that shed innocent blood,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A heart that deviseth wicked plots, feet that are swift to run into mischief,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A deceitful witness that uttereth lies, and him that soweth discord among brethren.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, keep the commandments of thy father, and forsake not the law of thy mother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Bind them in thy heart continually, and put them about thy neck.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When thou walkest, let them go with thee: when thou sleepest, let them keep thee, and when thou awakest, talk with them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because the commandment is a lamp, and the law a light, and reproofs of instruction are the way of life:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That they may keep thee from the evil woman, and from the flattering tongue of the stranger.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let not thy heart covet her beauty, be not caught with her winks:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the price of a harlot is scarce one loaf: but the woman catcheth the precious soul of a man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Can a man hide fire in his bosom, and his garments not burn?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Or can he walk upon hot coals, and his feet not be burnt?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So he that goeth in to his neighbour’s wife, shall not be clean when he shall touch her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fault is not so great when a man hath stolen: for he stealeth to fill his hungry soul:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if he be taken, he shall restore sevenfold, and shall give up all the substance of his house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he that is an adulterer, for the folly of his heart shall destroy his own soul:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He gathereth to himself shame and dishonour, and his reproach shall not be blotted out:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because the jealousy and rage of the husband will not spare in the day of revenge,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Nor will he yield to any man’s prayers, nor will he accept for satisfaction ever so many gifts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, keep my words, and lay up my precepts with thee. Son,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Keep my commandments, and thou shalt live: and my law as the apple of thy eye:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Bind it upon thy fingers, write it upon the tables of thy heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Say to wisdom: Thou art my sister: and call prudence thy friend,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That she may keep thee from the woman that is not thine, and from the stranger who sweeteneth her words.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For I looked out of the window of my house through the lattice,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I see little ones, I behold a foolish young man,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who passeth through the street by the corner, and goeth nigh the way of her house,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the dark when it grows late, in the darkness and obscurity of the night.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And behold a woman meeteth him in harlot’s attire, prepared to deceive souls: talkative and wandering,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Not bearing to be quiet, not able to abide still at home,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now abroad, now in the streets, now lying in wait near the corners.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And catching the young man, she kisseth him, and with an impudent face, flattereth, saying:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I vowed victims for prosperity, this day I have paid my vows.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore I am come out to meet thee, desirous to see thee, and I have found thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have woven my bed with cords, I have covered it with painted tapestry, brought from Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have perfumed my bed with myrrh, aloes, and cinnamon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Come, let us be inebriated with the breasts, and let us enjoy the desired embraces, till the day appear.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For my husband is not at home, he is gone a very long journey.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He took with him a bag of money: he will return home the day of the full moon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She entangled him with many words, and drew him away with the flattery of her lips.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Immediately he followeth her as an ox led to be a victim, and as a lamb playing the wanton, and not knowing that he is drawn like a fool to bonds,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Till the arrow pierce his liver: as if a bird should make haste to the snare, and knoweth not that his life is in danger.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now, therefore, my son, hear me, and attend to the words of my mouth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let not thy mind be drawn away in her ways: neither be thou deceived with her paths.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For she hath cast down many wounded, and the strongest have been slain by her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Her house is the way to hell, reaching even to the inner chambers of death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Doth not wisdom cry aloud, and prudence put forth her voice?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Standing in the top of the highest places by the way, in the midst of the paths,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Beside the gates of the city, in the very doors she speaketh, saying:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye men, to you I call, and my voice is to the sons of men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O little ones understand subtlety, and ye unwise, take notice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hear, for I will speak of great things: and my lips shall be opened to preach right things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My mouth shall meditate truth, and my lips shall hate wickedness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All my words are just, there is nothing wicked, nor perverse in them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They are right to them that understand, and just to them that find knowledge.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Receive my instruction, and not money: choose knowledge rather than gold.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For wisdom is better than all the most precious things: and whatsoever may be desired cannot be compared to it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I, wisdom, dwell in counsel, and am present in learned thoughts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fear of the Lord hateth evil; I hate arrogance, and pride, and every wicked way, and a mouth with a double tongue.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Counsel and equity is mine, prudence is mine, strength is mine.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By me kings reign, and lawgivers decree just things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By me princes rule, and the mighty decree justice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I love them that love me: and they that in the morning early watch for me, shall find me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With me are riches and glory, glorious riches and justice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For my fruit is better than gold and the precious stone, and my blossoms than choice silver.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I walk in the way of justice, in the midst of the paths of judgment,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That I may enrich them that love me, and may fill their treasures.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord possessed me in the beginning of his ways, before he made any thing from the beginning.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I was set up from eternity, and of old, before the earth was made.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The depths were not as yet, and I was already conceived, neither had the fountains of waters as yet sprung out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The mountains, with their huge bulk, had not as yet been established: before the hills, I was brought forth:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He had not yet made the earth, nor the rivers, nor the poles of the world.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When he prepared the heavens, I was present: when with a certain law, and compass, he enclosed the depths:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When he established the sky above, and poised the fountains of waters:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When he compassed the sea with its bounds, and set a law to the waters that they should not pass their limits: when he balanced the foundations of the earth;

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I was with him forming all things: and was delighted every day, playing before him at all times;

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Playing in the world: and my delights were to be with the children of men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now, therefore, ye children, hear me: blessed are they that keep my ways.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hear instruction, and be wise, and refuse it not.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Blessed is the man that heareth me, and that watcheth daily at my gates, and waiteth at the posts of my doors.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that shall find me, shall find life, and shall have salvation from the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he that shall sin against me shall hurt his own soul. All that hate me love death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wisdom hath built herself a house, she hath hewn her out seven pillars.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She hath slain her victims, mingled her wine, and set forth her table.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She hath sent her maids to invite to the tower, and to the walls of the city:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whosoever is a little one, let him come to me. And to the unwise she said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Come, eat my bread, and drink the wine which I have mingled for you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Forsake childishness, and live, and walk by the ways of prudence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that teacheth a scorner, doth an injury to himself; and he that rebuketh a wicked man, getteth himself a blot.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Rebuke not a scorner, lest he hate thee. Rebuke a wise man, and he will love thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give an occasion to a wise man, and wisdom shall be added to him. Teach a just man, and he shall make haste to receive it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom: and the knowledge of the holy is prudence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For by me shall thy days be multiplied, and years of life shall be added to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou be wise, thou shalt be so to thyself: and if a scorner, thou alone shalt bear the evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A foolish woman and clamorous, and full of allurements, and knowing nothing at all,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Sat at the door of her house, upon a seat, in a high place of the city,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To call them that pass by the way, and go on their journey:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that is a little one, let him turn to me. And to the fool she said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Stolen waters are sweeter, and hidden bread is more pleasant.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he did not know that giants are there, and that her guests are in the depths of hell.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wise son maketh the father glad: but a foolish son is the sorrow of his mother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Treasures of wickedness shall profit nothing: but justice shall deliver from death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord will not afflict the soul of the just with famine, and he will disappoint the deceitful practices of the wicked.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The slothful hand hath wrought poverty: but the hand of the industrious getteth riches. He that trusteth to lies feedeth the winds: and the same runneth after birds, that fly away.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that gathereth in the harvest, is a wise son: but he that snorteth in the summer, is the son of confusion.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The blessing of the Lord is upon the head of the just: but iniquity covereth the mouth of the wicked.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The memory of the just is with praises: and the name of the wicked shall rot.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wise of heart receiveth precepts: a fool is beaten with lips.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that walketh sincerely, walketh confidently: but he that perverteth his ways, shall be manifest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that winketh with the eye, shall cause sorrow: and the foolish in lips shall be beaten.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The mouth of the just is a vein of life: and the mouth of the wicked covereth iniquity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hatred stirreth up strifes: and charity covereth all sins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the lips of the wise is wisdom found: and a rod on the back of him that wanteth sense.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wise men lay up knowledge: but the mouth of the fool is next to confusion.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The substance of a rich man is the city of his strength: the fear of the poor is their poverty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The work of the just is unto life: but the fruit of the wicked unto sin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The way of life, to him that observeth correction: but he that forsaketh reproofs, goeth astray.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lying lips hide hatred: he that uttereth reproach, is foolish.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the multitude of words there shall not want sin: but he that refraineth his lips, is most wise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The tongue of the just is as choice silver: but the heart of the wicked is nothing worth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The lips of the just teach many: but they that are ignorant, shall die in the want of understanding.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The blessing of the Lord maketh men rich: neither shall affliction be joined to them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A fool worketh mischief as it were for sport: but wisdom is prudence to a man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That which the wicked feareth, shall come upon him: to the just their desire shall be given.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As a tempest that passeth, so the wicked shall be no more: but the just is as an everlasting foundation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As vinegar to the teeth, and smoke to the eyes, so is the sluggard to them that sent him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fear of the Lord shall prolong days: and the years of the wicked shall be shortened.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The expectation of the just is joy: but the hope of the wicked shall perish.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The strength of the upright is the way of the Lord: and fear to them that work evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The just shall never be moved: but the wicked shall not dwell on the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The mouth of the just shall bring forth wisdom: the tongue of the perverse shall perish.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The lips of the just consider what is acceptable: and the mouth of the wicked uttereth perverse things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A deceitful balance is an abomination before the Lord: and a just weight is his will.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Where pride is, there also shall be reproach: but where humility is, there also is wisdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The simplicity of the just shall guide them: and the deceitfulness of the wicked shall destroy them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Riches shall not profit in the day of revenge: but justice shall deliver from death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The justice of the upright shall make his way prosperous: and the wicked man shall fall by his own wickedness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The justice of the righteous shall deliver them: and the unjust shall be caught in their own snares.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When the wicked man is dead, there shall be no hope any more: and the expectation of the solicitous shall perish.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The just is delivered out of distress: and the wicked shall be given up for him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The dissembler with his mouth deceiveth his friend: but the just shall be delivered by knowledge.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When it goeth well with the just, the city shall rejoice: and when the wicked perish, there shall be praise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By the blessing of the just the city shall be exalted: and by the mouth of the wicked it shall be overthrown.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that despiseth his friend, is mean of heart: but the wise man will hold his peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that walketh deceitfully, revealeth secrets: but he that is faithful, concealeth the thing committed to him by his friend.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Where there is no governor, the people shall fall: but there is safety where there is much counsel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall be afflicted with evil, that is surety for a stranger: but he that is aware of snares, shall be secure.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A gracious woman shall find glory: and the strong shall have riches.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A merciful man doth good to his own soul: but he that is cruel casteth off even his own kindred.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wicked maketh an unsteady work: but to him that soweth justice, there is a faithful reward.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Clemency prepareth life: and the pursuing of evil things, death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A perverse heart is abominable to the Lord: and his will is in them that walk sincerely.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hand in hand the evil man shall not be innocent: but the seed of the just shall be saved.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A golden ring in a swine’s snout, a woman fair and foolish.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The desire of the just is all good, the expectation of the wicked is indignation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Some distribute their own goods, and grow richer: others take away what is not their own, and are always in want.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The soul that blesseth, shall be made fat: and he that inebriateth, shall be inebriated also himself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that hideth up corn, shall be cursed among the people: but a blessing upon the head of them that sell.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Well doth he rise early who seeketh good things; but he that seeketh after evil things, shall be oppressed by them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that trusteth in his riches shall fall: but the just shall spring up as a green leaf.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that troubleth his own house, shall inherit the winds: and the fool shall serve the wise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fruit of the just man is a tree of life: and he that gaineth souls is wise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If the just man receive in the earth, how much more the wicked and the sinner.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that loveth correction, loveth knowledge: but he that hateth reproof, is foolish.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that is good, shall draw grace from the Lord: but he that trusteth in his own devices, doth wickedly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Man shall not be strengthened by wickedness: and the root of the just shall not be moved.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A diligent woman is a crown to her husband: and she that doth things worthy of confusion, is as rottenness in his bones.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The thoughts of the just are judgments: and the counsels of the wicked are deceitful.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The words of the wicked lie in wait for blood: the mouth of the just shall deliver them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Turn the wicked, and they shall not be: but the house of the just shall stand firm.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man shall be known by his learning: but he that is vain and foolish, shall be exposed to contempt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Better is the poor man that provideth for himself, than he that is glorious and wanteth bread.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The just regardeth the lives of his beasts: but the bowels of the wicked are cruel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that tilleth his land shall be satisfied with bread: but he that pursueth idleness is very foolish.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that is delighted in passing his time over wine, leaveth a reproach in his strong holds. The desire of the wicked is the fortification of evil men: but the root of the just shall prosper.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the sins of the lips ruin draweth nigh to the evil man: but the just shall escape out of distress.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By the fruit of his own mouth shall a man be filled with good things, and according to the works of his hands it shall be repaid him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The way of a fool is right in his own eyes: but he that is wise hearkeneth unto counsels.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A fool immediately sheweth his anger: but he that dissembleth injuries is wise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that speaketh that which he knoweth, sheweth forth justice: but he that lieth, is a deceitful witness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is that promiseth, and is pricked as it were with a sword of conscience: but the tongue of the wise is health.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The lip of truth shall be steadfast for ever: but he that is a hasty witness, frameth a lying tongue.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Deceit is in the heart of them that think evil things: but joy followeth them that take counsels of peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whatsoever shall befall the just man, shall not make him sad: but the wicked shall be filled with mischief.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lying lips are an abomination to the Lord: but they that deal faithfully, please him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A cautious man concealeth knowledge: and the heart of fools publisheth folly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The hand of the valiant shall bear rule: but that which is slothful shall be under tribute.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Grief in the heart of a man shall bring him low, but with a good word he shall be made glad.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that neglecteth a loss for the sake of a friend, is just: but the way of the wicked shall deceive them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The deceitful man shall not find gain: but the substance of a just man shall be precious gold.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the path of justice is life: but the bye-way leadeth to death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wise son heareth the doctrine of his father: but he that is a scorner, heareth not when he is reproved.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Of the fruit of his own mouth shall a man be filled with good things: but the soul of transgressors is wicked.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that keepeth his mouth keepeth his soul: but he that hath no guard on his speech shall meet with evils.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sluggard willeth, and willeth not: but the soul of them that work, shall be made fat.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The just shall hate a lying word: but the wicked confoundeth, and shall be confounded.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Justice keepeth the way of the innocent: but wickedness overthroweth the sinner.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: One is as it were rich, when he hath nothing and another is as it were poor, when he hath great riches.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The ransom of a man’s life are his riches: but he that is poor, beareth not reprehension.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The light of the just giveth joy: but the lamp of the wicked shall be put out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Among the proud there are always contentions: but they that do all things with counsel, are ruled by wisdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Substance got in haste shall be diminished: but that which by little and little is gathered with the hand, shall increase.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hope that is deferred afflicteth the soul: desire when it cometh, is a tree of life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whosoever speaketh ill of any thing, bindeth himself for the time to come: but he that feareth the commandment, shall dwell in peace. Deceitful souls go astray in sins: the just are merciful, and shew mercy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The law of the wise is a fountain of life, that he may decline from the ruin of death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Good instruction shall give grace: in the way of scorners is a deep pit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The prudent man doth all things with counsel: but he that is a fool, layeth open his folly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The messenger of the wicked shall fall into mischief: but a faithful ambassador is health.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Poverty and shame to him that refuseth instruction: but he that yieldeth to reproof shall be glorified.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The desire that is accomplished, delighteth the soul: fools hate them that flee from evil things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that walketh with the wise, shall be wise: a friend of fools shall become like to them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Evil pursueth sinners: and to the just good shall be repaid.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The good man leaveth heirs, sons, and grandsons: and the substance of the sinner is kept for the just.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Much food is in the tillage of fathers: but for others it is gathered without judgment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that spareth the rod, hateth his son: but he that loveth him, correcteth him betimes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The just eateth and filleth his soul: but the belly of the wicked is never to be filled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wise woman buildeth her house: but the foolish will pull down with her hands that also which is built.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that walketh in the right way, and feareth God, is despised by him that goeth by an infamous way.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the mouth of a fool is the rod of pride: but the lips of the wise preserve them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Where there are no oxen, the crib is empty: but where there is much corn, there the strength of the ox is manifest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A faithful witness will not lie: but a deceitful witness uttereth a lie.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A scorner seeketh wisdom, and findeth it not: the learning of the wise is easy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Go against a foolish man, and he knoweth not the lips of prudence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wisdom of a discreet man is to understand his way: and the imprudence of fools erreth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A fool will laugh at sin, but among the just grace shall abide.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The heart that knoweth the bitterness of his own soul, in his joy the stranger shall not intermeddle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The house of the wicked shall be destroyed: but the tabernacles of the just shall flourish.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is a way which seemeth just to a man: but the ends thereof lead to death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Laughter shall be mingled with sorrow, and mourning taketh hold of the ends of joy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A fool shall be filled with his own ways, and the good man shall be above him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The innocent believeth every word: the discreet man considereth his steps. No good shall come to the deceitful son: but the wise servant shall prosper in his dealings, and his way shall be made straight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wise man feareth, and declineth from evil: the fool leapeth over, and is confident.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The impatient man shall work folly: and the crafty man is hateful.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The childish shall possess folly, and the prudent shall look for knowledge.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The evil shall fall down before the good: and the wicked before the gates of the just.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The poor man shall be hateful even to his own neighbour: but the friends of the rich are many.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that despiseth his neighbour, sinneth: but he that sheweth mercy to the poor, shall be blessed. He that believeth in the Lord, loveth mercy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They err that work evil: but mercy and truth prepare good things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In much work there shall be abundance: but where there are many words, there is oftentimes want.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The crown of the wise, is their riches: the folly of fools, imprudence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A faithful witness delivereth souls: and the double dealer uttereth lies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the fear of the Lord is confidence of strength, and there shall be hope for his children.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fear of the Lord is a fountain of life, to decline from the ruin of death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the multitude of people is the dignity of the king: and in the small number of the people the dishonour of the prince.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that is patient, is governed with much wisdom: but he that is impatient, exalteth his folly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Soundness of heart is the life of the flesh: but envy is the rottenness of the bones.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that oppresseth the poor, upbraideth his maker: but he that hath pity on the poor, honoureth him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wicked man shall be driven out in his wickedness: but the just hath hope in his death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the heart of the prudent resteth wisdom, and it shall instruct all the ignorant.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Justice exalteth a nation: but sin maketh nations miserable.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wise servant is acceptable to the king: he that is good for nothing shall feel his anger.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A mild answer breaketh wrath: but a harsh word stirreth up fury.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The tongue of the wise adorneth knowledge: but the mouth of fools bubbleth out folly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The eyes of the Lord in every place behold the good and the evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A peaceable tongue is a tree of life: but that which is immoderate, shall crush the spirit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A fool laugheth at the instruction of his father: but he that regardeth reproofs shall become prudent. In abundant justice there is the greatest strength: but the devices of the wicked shall be rooted out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The house of the just is very much strength: and in the fruits of the wicked is trouble.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The lips of the wise shall disperse knowledge: the heart of fools shall be unlike.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The victims of the wicked are abominable to the Lord: the vows of the just are acceptable.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The way of the wicked is an abomination to the Lord: he that followeth justice is beloved by him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Instruction is grievous to him that forsaketh the way of life: he that hateth reproof shall die.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hell and destruction are before the Lord: how much more the hearts of the children of men?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A corrupt man loveth not one that reproveth him: nor will he go to the wise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A glad heart maketh a cheerful countenance: but by grief of mind the spirit is cast down.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The heart of the wise seeketh instruction: and the mouth of fools feedeth on foolishness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All the days of the poor are evil: a secure mind is like a continual feast.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Better is a little with the fear of the Lord, than great treasures without content.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is better to be invited to herbs with love, than to a fatted calf with hatred.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A passionate man stirreth up strifes: he that is patient appeaseth those that are stirred up.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The way of the slothful is as a hedge of thorns: the way of the just is without offence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wise son maketh a father joyful: but the foolish man despiseth his mother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Folly is joy to the fool: and the wise man maketh straight his steps.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Designs are brought to nothing where there is no counsel: but where there are many counsellors, they are established.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man rejoiceth in the sentence of his mouth: and a word in due time is best.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The path of life is above for the wise, that he may decline from the lowest hell.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord will destroy the house of the proud: and will strengthen the borders of the widow.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Evil thoughts are an abomination to the Lord: and pure words most beautiful shall be confirmed by him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that is greedy of gain troubleth his own house: but he that hateth bribes shall live. By mercy and faith sins are purged away: and by the fear of the Lord every one declineth from evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The mind of the just studieth obedience: the mouth of the wicked overfloweth with evils.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner
