text: The Lord is far from the wicked: and he will hear the prayers of the just.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The light of the eyes rejoiceth the soul: a good name maketh the bones fat.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The ear that heareth the reproofs of life, shall abide in the midst of the wise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that rejecteth instruction, despiseth his own soul: but he that yieldeth to reproof, possesseth understanding.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fear of the Lord is the lesson of wisdom: and humility goeth before glory.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is the part of man to prepare the soul: and of the Lord to govern the tongue.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All the ways of a man are open to his eyes: the Lord is the weigher of spirits.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lay open thy works to the Lord: and thy thoughts shall be directed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord hath made all things for himself: the wicked also for the evil day.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Every proud man is an abomination to the Lord: though hand should be joined to hand, he is not innocent. The beginning of a good way is to do justice: and this is more acceptable with God, than to offer sacrifices.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By mercy and truth iniquity is redeemed; and by the fear of the Lord men depart from evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When the ways of man shall please the Lord, he will convert even his enemies to peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Better is a little with justice, than great revenues with iniquity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The heart of man disposeth his way: but the Lord must direct his steps.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Divination is in the lips of the king, his mouth shall not err in judgment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Weight and balance are judgments of the Lord: and his work all the weights of the bag.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They that act wickedly are abominable to the king: for the throne is established by justice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Just lips are the delight of kings: he that speaketh right things shall be loved.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wrath of a king is as messengers of death: and the wise man will pacify it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the cheerfulness of the king’s countenance is life: and his clemency is like the latter rain.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Get wisdom, because it is better than gold: and purchase prudence, for it is more precious than silver.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The path of the just departeth from evils: he that keepeth his soul keepeth his way.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Pride goeth before destruction: and the spirit is lifted up before a fall.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is better to be humbled with the meek, than to divide spoils with the proud.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The learned in word shall find good things: and he that trusteth in the Lord is blessed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wise in heart shall be called prudent: and he that is sweet in words, shall attain to greater things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Knowledge is a fountain of life to him that possesseth it: the instruction of fools is foolishness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The heart of the wise shall instruct his mouth: and shall add grace to his lips.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Well ordered words are as a honeycomb: sweet to the soul, and health to the bones.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is a way that seemeth to a man right: and the ends thereof lead to death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The soul of him that laboureth, laboureth for himself, because his mouth hath obliged him to it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wicked man diggeth evil, and in his lips is a burning fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A perverse man stirreth up quarrels: and one full of words separateth princes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: An unjust man allureth his friend: and leadeth him into a way that is not good.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that with fixed eyes deviseth wicked things, biting his lips, bringeth evil to pass.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Old age is a crown of dignity, when it is found in the ways of justice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The patient man is better than the valiant: and he that ruleth his spirit, than he that taketh cities.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lots are cast into the lap, but they are disposed of by the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Better is a dry morsel with joy, than a house full of victims with strife.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wise servant shall rule over foolish sons, and shall divide the inheritance among the brethren.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As silver is tried by fire, and gold in the furnace: so the Lord trieth the hearts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The evil man obeyeth an unjust tongue: and the deceitful hearkeneth to lying lips.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that despiseth the poor, reproacheth his maker: and he that rejoiceth at another man’s ruin, shall not be unpunished.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Children’s children are the crown of old men: and the glory of children are their fathers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Eloquent words do not become a fool, nor lying lips a prince.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The expectation of him that expecteth is a most acceptable jewel: whithersoever he turneth himself, he understandeth wisely.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that concealeth a transgression, seeketh friendships: he that repeateth it again, separateth friends.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A reproof availeth more with a wise man, than a hundred stripes with a fool.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: An evil man always seeketh quarrels: but a cruel angel shall be sent against him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is better to meet a bear robbed of her whelps, than a fool trusting in his own folly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that rendereth evil for good, evil shall not depart from his house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The beginning of quarrels is as when one letteth out water: and before he suffereth reproach, he forsaketh judgment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that justifieth the wicked, and he that condemneth the just, both are abominable before God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What doth it avail a fool to have riches, seeing he cannot buy wisdom? He that maketh his house high, seeketh a downfall: and he that refuseth to learn, shall fall into evils.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that is a friend loveth at all times: and a brother is proved in distress.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A foolish man will clap hands, when he is surety for his friend.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that studieth discords, loveth quarrels: and he that exalteth his door, seeketh ruin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that is of a perverse heart, shall not find good: and he that perverteth his tongue, shall fall into evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A fool is born to his own disgrace: and even his father shall not rejoice in a fool.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A joyful mind maketh age flourishing: a sorrowful spirit drieth up the bones.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wicked man taketh gifts out of the bosom, that he may pervert the paths of judgment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wisdom shineth in the face of the wise: the eyes of fools are in the ends of the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A foolish son is the anger of the father: and the sorrow of the mother that bore him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is no good thing to do hurt to the just: nor to strike the prince, who judgeth right.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that setteth bounds to his words, is knowing and wise: and the man of understanding is of a precious spirit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Even a fool, if he will hold his peace, shall be counted wise: and if he close his lips, a man of understanding.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that hath a mind to depart from a friend, seeketh occasions: he shall ever be subject to reproach.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A fool receiveth not the words of prudence: unless thou say those things which are in his heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wicked man, when he is come into the depth of sins, contemneth: but ignominy and reproach follow him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Words from the mouth of a man are as deep water: and the fountain of wisdom is an overflowing stream.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is not good to accept the person of the wicked, to decline from the truth of judgment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The lips of a fool intermeddle with strife: and his mouth provoketh quarrels.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The mouth of a fool is his destruction: and his lips are the ruin of his soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The words of the double tongued are as if they were harmless: and they reach even to the inner parts of the bowels. Fear casteth down the slothful: and the souls of the effeminate shall be hungry.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that is loose and slack in his work, is the brother of him that wasteth his own works.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The name of the Lord is a strong tower: the just runneth to it, and shall be exalted.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The substance of the rich man is the city of his strength, and as a strong wall compassing him about.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Before destruction, the heart of a man is exalted: and before he be glorified, it is humbled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that answereth before he heareth, sheweth himself to be a fool, and worthy of confusion.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The spirit of a man upholdeth his infirmity: but a spirit that is easily angered, who can bear?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wise heart shall acquire knowledge: and the ear of the wise seeketh instruction.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man’s gift enlargeth his way, and maketh him room before princes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The just is first accuser of himself: his friend cometh, and shall search him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The lot suppresseth contentions, and determineth even between the mighty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A brother that is helped by his brother, is like a strong city: and judgments are like the bars of cities.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Of the fruit of a man’s mouth shall his belly be satisfied: and the offspring of his lips shall fill him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Death and life are in the power of the tongue: they that love it, shall eat the fruits thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that hath found a good wife, hath found a good thing, and shall receive a pleasure from the Lord. He that driveth away a good wife, driveth away a good thing: but he that keepeth an adulteress, is foolish and wicked.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The poor will speak with supplications, and the rich will speak roughly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man amiable in society, shall be more friendly than a brother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Better is the poor man, that walketh in his simplicity, than a rich man that is perverse in his lips and unwise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Where there is no knowledge of the soul, there is no good: and he that is hasty with his feet shall stumble.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The folly of a man supplanteth his steps: and he fretteth in his mind against God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Riches make many friends: but from the poor man, even they whom he had, depart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A false witness shall not be unpunished: and he that speaketh lies, shall not escape.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Many honour the person of him that is mighty, and are friends of him that giveth gifts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The brethren of the poor man hate him: moreover also his friends have departed far from him. He that followeth after words only, shall have nothing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he that possesseth a mind, loveth his own soul, and he that keepeth prudence, shall find good things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A false witness shall not be unpunished: and he that speaketh lies, shall perish.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Delicacies are not seemly for a fool: nor for a servant to have rule over princes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The learning of a man is known by patience: and his glory is to pass over wrongs.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As the roaring of a lion, so also is the anger of a king: and his cheerfulness as the dew upon the grass.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A foolish son is the grief of his father: and a wrangling wife is like a roof continually dropping through.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: House and riches are given by parents: but a prudent wife is properly from the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Slothfulness casteth into a deep sleep, and an idle soul shall suffer hunger.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that keepeth the commandment, keepeth his own soul: but he that neglecteth his own way, shall die.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that hath mercy on the poor, lendeth to the Lord: and he will repay him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Chastise thy son, despair not: but to the killing of him set not thy soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that is impatient, shall suffer damage: and when he shall take away, he shall add another thing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hear counsel, and receive instruction, that thou mayst be wise in thy latter end.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There are many thoughts in the heart of a man: but the will of the Lord shall stand firm.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A needy man is merciful: and better is the poor than the lying man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fear of the Lord is unto life: and he shall abide in the fulness without being visited with evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The slothful hideth his hand under his armpit, and will not so much as bring it to his mouth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wicked man being scourged, the fool shall be wiser: but if thou rebuke a wise man, he will understand discipline.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that afflicteth his father, and chaseth away his mother, is infamous and unhappy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Cease not, O my son, to hear instruction, and be not ignorant of the words of knowledge.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: An unjust witness scorneth judgment: and the mouth of the wicked devoureth iniquity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Judgments are prepared for scorners: and striking hammers for the bodies of fools.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wine is a luxurious thing, and drunkenness riotous: whosoever is delighted therewith, shall not be wise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As the roaring of a lion, so also is the dread of a king: he that provoketh him, sinneth against his own soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is an honour for a man to separate himself from quarrels: but all fools are meddling with reproaches.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because of the cold the sluggard would not plough: he shall beg therefore in the summer, and it shall not be given him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Counsel in the heart of a man is like deep water: but a wise man will draw it out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Many men are called merciful: but who shall find a faithful man?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The just that walketh in his simplicity, shall leave behind him blessed children.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The king, that sitteth on the throne of judgment, scattereth away all evil with his look.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who can say: My heart is clean, I am pure from sin?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Diverse weights and diverse measures, both are abominable before God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By his inclinations a child is known, if his works be clean and right.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The hearing ear, and the seeing eye, the Lord hath made them both.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Love not sleep, lest poverty oppress thee: open thy eyes, and be filled with bread.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is naught, it is naught, saith every buyer: and when he is gone away, then he will boast.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is gold and a multitude of jewels: but the lips of knowledge are a precious vessel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Take away the garment of him that is surety for a stranger, and take a pledge from him for strangers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The bread of lying is sweet to a man: but afterwards his mouth shall be filled with gravel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Designs are strengthened by counsels: and wars are to be managed by governments.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Meddle not with him that revealeth secrets, and walketh deceitfully, and openeth wide his lips.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that curseth his father, and mother, his lamp shall be put out in the midst of darkness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The inheritance gotten hastily in the beginning, in the end shall be without a blessing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Say not: I will return evil: wait for the Lord, and he will deliver thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Diverse weights are an abomination before the Lord: a deceitful balance is not good.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The steps of men are guided by the Lord: but who is the man that can understand his own way?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is ruin to a man to devour holy ones, and after vows to retract.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wise king scattereth the wicked, and bringeth over them the wheel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The spirit of a man is the lamp of the Lord, which searcheth all the hidden things of the bowels.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Mercy and truth preserve the king, and his throne is strengthened by clemency.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The joy of young men is their strength: and the dignity of old men, their grey hairs.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The blueness of a wound shall wipe away evils: and stripes in the more inward parts of the belly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As the divisions of waters, so the heart of the king is in the hand of the Lord: whithersoever he will, he shall turn it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Every way of a man seemeth right to himself: but the Lord weigheth the hearts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To do mercy and judgment, pleaseth the Lord more than victims.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Haughtiness of the eyes is the enlarging of the heart: the lamp of the wicked is sin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The thoughts of the industrious always bring forth abundance: but every sluggard is always in want.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that gathereth treasures by a lying tongue, is vain and foolish, and shall stumble upon the snares of death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The robberies of the wicked shall be their downfall, because they would not do judgment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The perverse way of a man is strange: but as for him that is pure, his work is right.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is better to sit in a corner of the housetop, than with a brawling woman, and in a common house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The soul of the wicked desireth evil, he will not have pity on his neighbour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When a pestilent man is punished, the little one will be wiser: and if he follow the wise, he will receive knowledge.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The just considereth seriously the house of the wicked, that he may withdraw the wicked from evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that stoppeth his ear against the cry of the poor, shall also cry himself, and shall not be heard.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A secret present quencheth anger: and a gift in the bosom, the greatest wrath.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is joy to the just to do judgment: and dread to them that work iniquity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man that shall wander out of the way of doctrine, shall abide in the company of the giants.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that loveth good cheer, shall be in want: he that loveth wine, and fat things, shall not be rich.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wicked is delivered up for the just: and the unjust for the righteous.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is better to dwell in a wilderness, than with a quarrelsome and passionate woman.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is a treasure to be desired, and oil in the dwelling of the just: and the foolish man shall spend it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that followeth justice and mercy, shall find life, justice, and glory.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wise man hath scaled the city of the strong, and hath cast down the strength of the confidence thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that keepeth his mouth and his tongue, keepeth his soul from distress.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The proud and the arrogant is called ignorant, who in anger worketh pride.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Desires kill the slothful: for his hands have refused to work at all.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He longeth and desireth all the day: but he that is just, will give, and will not cease.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sacrifices of the wicked are abominable, because they are offered of wickedness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A lying witness shall perish: an obedient man shall speak of victory.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wicked man impudently hardeneth his face: but he that is righteous, correcteth his way.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is no wisdom, there is no prudence, there is no counsel against the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The horse is prepared for the day of battle: but the Lord giveth safety.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A good name is better than great riches: and good favour is above silver and gold.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The rich and poor have met one another: the Lord is the maker of them both.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The prudent man saw the evil, and hid himself: the simple passed on, and suffered loss.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fruit of humility is the fear of the Lord, riches and glory and life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Arms and swords are in the way of the perverse: but he that keepeth his own soul, departeth far from them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is a proverb: A young man according to his way, even when he is old, he will not depart from it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The rich ruleth over the poor: and the borrower is servant to him that lendeth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that soweth iniquity, shall reap evils, and with the rod of his anger he shall be consumed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that is inclined to mercy, shall be blessed: for of his bread he hath given to the poor. He that maketh presents, shall purchase victory and honour: but he carrieth away the souls of the receivers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Cast out the scoffer, and contention shall go out with him, and quarrels and reproaches shall cease.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that loveth cleanness of heart, for the grace of his lips shall have the king for his friend.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The eyes of the Lord preserve knowledge: and the words of the unjust are overthrown.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The slothful man saith: There is a lion without, I shall be slain in the midst of the streets.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The mouth of a strange woman is a deep pit: he whom the Lord is angry with, shall fall into it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Folly is bound up in the heart of a child, and the rod of correction shall drive it away.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that oppresseth the poor, to increase his own riches, shall himself give to one that is richer, and shall be in need.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Incline thy ear, and hear the words of the wise: and apply thy heart to my doctrine:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Which shall be beautiful for thee, if thou keep it in thy bowels, and it shall flow in thy lips:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That thy trust may be in the Lord, wherefore I have also shewn it to thee this day.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold I have described it to thee three manner of ways, in thoughts and knowledge:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That I might shew thee the certainty, and the words of truth, to answer out of these to them that sent thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Do no violence to the poor, because he is poor: and do not oppress the needy in the gate:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because the Lord will judge his cause: and will afflict them that have afflicted his soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not a friend to an angry man, and do not walk with a furious man:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lest perhaps thou learn his ways, and take scandal to thy soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not with them that fasten down their hands, and that offer themselves sureties for debts:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For if thou have not wherewith to restore, what cause is there that he should take the covering from thy bed?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Pass not beyond the ancient bounds which thy fathers have set.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hast thou seen a man swift in his work? he shall stand before kings, and shall not be before those that are obscure.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When thou shalt sit to eat with a prince, consider diligently what is set before thy face:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And put a knife to thy throat, if it be so that thou have thy soul in thy own power.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not desirous of his meats, in which is the bread of deceit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Labour not to be rich: but set bounds to thy prudence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lift not up thy eyes to riches which thou canst not have: because they shall make themselves wings like those of an eagle, and shall fly towards heaven.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Eat not with an envious man, and desire not his meats:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because, like a soothsayer, and diviner, he thinketh that which he knoweth not. Eat and drink, will he say to thee: and his mind is not with thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The meats which thou hadst eaten, thou shalt vomit up: and shalt loose thy beautiful words.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Speak not in the ears of fools: because they will despise the instruction of thy speech.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Touch not the bounds of little ones: and enter not into the field of the fatherless:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For their near kinsman is strong: and he will judge their cause against thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let thy heart apply itself to instruction and thy ears to words of knowledge.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Withhold not correction from a child: for if thou strike him with the rod, he shall not die.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou shalt beat him with the rod, and deliver his soul from hell.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, if thy mind be wise, my heart shall rejoice with thee:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And my reins shall rejoice, when thy lips shall speak what is right.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let not thy heart envy sinners: but be thou in the fear of the Lord all the day long:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because thou shalt have hope in the latter end, and thy expectation shall not be taken away.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hear thou, my son, and be wise: and guide thy mind in the way.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not in the feasts of great drinkers, nor in their revellings, who contribute flesh to eat:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because they that give themselves to drinking, and that club together, shall be consumed: and drowsiness shall be clothed with rags.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hearken to thy father, that begot thee: and despise not thy mother when she is old.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Buy truth, and do not sell wisdom, and instruction, and understanding.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The father of the just rejoiceth greatly: he that hath begotten a wise son, shall have joy in him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let thy father and thy mother be joyful, and let her rejoice that bore thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, give me thy heart: and let thy eyes keep my ways.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For a harlot is a deep ditch: and a strange woman is a narrow pit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She lieth in wait in the way as a robber, and him whom she shall see unwary, she will kill.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who hath woe? whose father hath woe? who hath contentions? who falls into pits? who hath wounds without cause? who hath redness of eyes?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Surely they that pass their time in wine, and study to drink off their cups.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Look not upon the wine when it is yellow, when the colour thereof shineth in the glass: it goeth in pleasantly,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But in the end, it will bite like a snake, and will spread abroad poison like a basilisk.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy eyes shall behold strange women, and thy heart shall utter perverse things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thou shalt be as one sleeping in the midst of the sea, and as a pilot fast asleep when the stern is lost.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thou shalt say: They have beaten me, but I was not sensible of pain: they drew me, and I felt not: when shall I awake and find wine again?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Seek not to be like evil men, neither desire to be with them:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because their mind studieth robberies, and their lips speak deceits.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By wisdom the house shall be built, and by prudence it shall be strengthened.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By instruction the storerooms shall be filled with all precious and most beautiful wealth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wise man is strong: and a knowing man, stout and valiant.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because war is managed by due ordering: and there shall be safety where there are many counsels.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wisdom is too high for a fool; in the gate he shall not open his mouth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that deviseth to do evils, shall be called a fool.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The thought of a fool is sin: and the detractor is the abomination of men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou lose hope, being weary in the day of distress, thy strength shall be diminished.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Deliver them that are led to death: and those that are drawn to death, forbear not to deliver.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou say: I have not strength enough: he that seeth into the heart, he understandeth, and nothing deceiveth the keeper of thy soul, and he shall render to a man according to his works.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Eat honey, my son, because it is good, and the honeycomb most sweet to thy throat.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So also is the doctrine of wisdom to thy soul: which when thou hast found, thou shalt have hope in the end, and thy hope shall not perish.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lie not in wait, nor seek after wickedness in the house of the just, nor spoil his rest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For a just man shall fall seven times, and shall rise again: but the wicked shall fall down into evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When thy enemy shall fall, be not glad, and in his ruin let not thy heart rejoice:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lest the Lord see, and it displease him, and he turn away his wrath from him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Contend not with the wicked, nor seek to be like the ungodly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For evil men have no hope of things to come, and the lamp of the wicked shall be put out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, fear the Lord, and the king: and have nothing to do with detractors.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For their destruction shall rise suddenly: and who knoweth the ruin of both?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These things also to the wise: It is not good to have respect to persons in judgment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They that say to the wicked man: Thou art just: shall be cursed by the people, and the tribes shall abhor them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They that rebuke him shall be praised: and a blessing shall come upon them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall kiss the lips, who answereth right words.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Prepare thy work without, and diligently till thy ground: that afterward thou mayst build thy house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not witness without cause against thy neighbour: and deceive not any man with thy lips.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Say not: I will do to him as he hath done to me: I will render to every one according to his work.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I passed by the field of the slothful man, and by the vineyard of the foolish man:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And behold it was all filled with nettles, and thorns had covered the face thereof, and the stone wall was broken down.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Which when I had seen, I laid it up in my heart, and by the example I received instruction.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou wilt sleep a little, said I, thou wilt slumber a little, thou wilt fold thy hands a little to rest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And poverty shall come to thee as a runner, and beggary as an armed man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These are also parables of Solomon, which the men of Ezechias, king of Juda, copied out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is the glory of God to conceal the word, and the glory of kings to search out the speech.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The heaven above and the earth beneath, and the heart of kings is unsearchable.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Take away the rust from silver, and there shall come forth a most pure vessel:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Take away wickedness from the face of the king, and his throne shall be established with justice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Appear not glorious before the king, and stand not in the place of great men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For it is better that it should be said to thee: Come up hither; than that thou shouldst be humbled before the prince.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The things which thy eyes have seen, utter not hastily in a quarrel: lest afterward thou mayst not be able to make amends, when thou hast dishonoured thy friend.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Treat thy cause with thy friend, and discover not the secret to a stranger:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lest he insult over thee, when he hath heard it, and cease not to upbraid thee. Grace and friendship deliver a man: keep these for thyself, lest thou fall under reproach.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To speak a word in due time, is like apples of gold on beds of silver.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As an earring of gold and a bright pearl, so is he that reproveth the wise, and the obedient ear.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As the cold of snow in the time of harvest, so is a faithful messenger to him that sent him, for he refresheth his soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As clouds, and wind, when no rain followeth, so is the man that boasteth, and doth not fulfil his promises.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By patience a prince shall be appeased, and a soft tongue shall break hardness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou hast found honey, eat what is sufficient for thee, lest being glutted therewith thou vomit it up.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Withdraw thy foot from the house of thy neighbour, lest having his fill he hate thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man that beareth false witness against his neighbour, is like a dart and a sword and a sharp arrow.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To trust in an unfaithful man in the time of trouble, is like a rotten tooth, and weary foot,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And one that looseth his garment in cold weather. As vinegar upon nitre, so is he that singeth songs to a very evil heart. As a moth doth by a garment, and a worm by the wood: so the sadness of a man consumeth the heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thy enemy be hungry, give him to eat: if he thirst, give him water to drink:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For thou shalt heap hot coals upon his head, and the Lord will reward thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The north wind driveth away rain, as doth a sad countenance a backbiting tongue.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is better to sit in a corner of the housetop: than with a brawling woman, and in a common house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As cold water to a thirsty soul, so are good tidings from a far country.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A just man falling down before the wicked, is as a fountain troubled with the foot and a corrupted spring.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As it is not good for a man to eat much honey, so he that is a searcher of majesty shall be overwhelmed by glory.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As a city that lieth open and is not compassed with walls, so is a man that cannot refrain his own spirit in speaking.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As snow in summer, and rain in harvest, so glory is not seemly for a fool.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As a bird flying to other places, and a sparrow going here or there: so a curse uttered without cause shall come upon a man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A whip for a horse, and a snaffle for an ass, and a rod for the back of fools.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Answer not a fool according to his folly, lest thou be made like him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Answer a fool according to his folly, lest he imagine himself to be wise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that sendeth words by a foolish messenger, is lame of feet and drinketh iniquity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As a lame man hath fair legs in vain: so a parable is unseemly in the mouth of fools.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As he that casteth a stone into the heap of Mercury: so is he that giveth honour to a fool.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As if a thorn should grow in the hand of a drunkard: so is a parable in the mouth of fools.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Judgment determineth causes: and he that putteth a fool to silence, appeaseth anger.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As a dog that returneth to his vomit, so is the fool that repeateth his folly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hast thou seen a man wise in his own conceit? there shall be more hope of a fool than of him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The slothful man saith: There is a lion in the way, and a lioness in the roads.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As the door turneth upon its hinges, so doth the slothful upon his bed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The slothful hideth his hand under his armpit, and it grieveth him to turn it to his mouth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sluggard is wiser in his own conceit, than seven men that speak sentences.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As he that taketh a dog by the ears, so is he that passeth by in anger, and meddleth with another man’s quarrel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As he is guilty that shooteth arrows, and lances unto death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So is the man that hurteth his friend deceitfully: and when he is taken, saith: I did it in jest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When the wood faileth, the fire shall go out: and when the talebearer is taken away, contentions shall cease.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As coals are to burning coals, and wood to fire, so an angry man stirreth up strife.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The words of a talebearer are as it were simple, but they reach to the innermost parts of the belly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Swelling lips joined with a corrupt heart, are like an earthen vessel adorned with silver dross.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: An enemy is known by his lips, when in his heart he entertaineth deceit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When he shall speak low, trust him not: because there are seven mischiefs in his heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that covereth hatred deceitfully, his malice shall be laid open in the public assembly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that diggeth a pit, shall fall into it: and he that rolleth a stone, it shall return to him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A deceitful tongue loveth not truth: and a slippery mouth worketh ruin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Boast not for to morrow, for thou knowest not what the day to come may bring forth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let another praise thee, and not thy own mouth: a stranger, and not thy own lips.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A stone is heavy, and sand weighty: but the anger of a fool is heavier than them both.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Anger hath no mercy: nor fury, when it breaketh forth: and who can bear the violence of one provoked?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Open rebuke is better than hidden love.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Better are the wounds of a friend, than the deceitful kisses of an enemy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A soul that is full shall tread upon the honeycomb: and a soul that is hungry shall take even bitter for sweet.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As a bird that wandereth from her nest, so is a man that leaveth his place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Ointment and perfumes rejoice the heart: and the good counsels of a friend are sweet to the soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy own friend, and thy father’s friend, forsake not: and go not into thy brother’s house in the day of thy affliction. Better is a neighbour that is near than a brother afar off.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Study wisdom, my son, and make my heart joyful, that thou mayst give an answer to him that reproacheth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The prudent man seeing evil hideth himself: little ones passing on have suffered losses.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Take away his garment that hath been surety for a stranger: and take from him a pledge for strangers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that blesseth his neighbour with a loud voice, rising in the night, shall be like to him that curseth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Roofs dropping through in a cold day, and a contentious woman are alike.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that retaineth her, is as he that would hold the wind, and shall call the oil of his right hand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Iron sharpeneth iron, so a man sharpeneth the countenance of his friend.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that keepeth the fig tree, shall eat the fruit thereof: and he that is the keeper of his master, shall be glorified.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As the faces of them that look therein, shine in the water, so the hearts of men are laid open to the wise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hell and destruction are never filled: so the eyes of men are never satisfied.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As silver is tried in the fining-pot, and gold in the furnace: so a man is tried by the mouth of him that praiseth. The heart of the wicked seeketh after evils, but the righteous heart seeketh after knowledge.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Though thou shouldst bray a fool in the mortar, as when a pestle striketh upon sodden barley, his folly would not be taken from him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be diligent to know the countenance of thy cattle, and consider thy own flocks:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For thou shalt not always have power: but a crown shall be given to generation and generation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The meadows are open, and the green herbs have appeared, and the hay is gathered out of the mountains.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lambs are for thy clothing: and kids for the price of the field.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let the milk of the goats be enough for thy food, and for the necessities of thy house, and for maintenance for thy handmaids.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wicked man fleeth, when no man pursueth: but the just, bold as a lion, shall be without dread.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the sins of the land many are the princes thereof: and for the wisdom of a man, and the knowledge of those things that are said, the life of the prince shall be prolonged.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A poor man that oppresseth the poor, is like a violent shower, which bringeth a famine.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They that forsake the law, praise the wicked man: they that keep it, are incensed against him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Evil men think not on judgment: but they that seek after the Lord, take notice of all things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Better is the poor man walking in his simplicity, than the rich in crooked ways.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that keepeth the law, is a wise son: but he that feedeth gluttons, shameth his father.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that heapeth together riches by usury and loan, gathereth them for him that will be bountiful to the poor.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that turneth away his ears from hearing the law, his prayer shall be an abomination.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that deceiveth the just in a wicked way, shall fall in his own destruction: and the upright shall possess his goods.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The rich man seemeth to himself wise: but the poor man that is prudent shall search him out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the joy of the just there is great glory: when the wicked reign, men are ruined.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that hideth his sins, shall not prosper: but he that shall confess, and forsake them, shall obtain mercy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Blessed is the man that is always fearful: but he that is hardened in mind shall fall into evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As a roaring lion, and a hungry bear, so is a wicked prince over the poor people.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A prince void of prudence shall oppress many by calumny: but he that hateth covetousness, shall prolong his days.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man that doth violence to the blood of a person, if he flee even to the pit, no man will stay him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that walketh uprightly, shall be saved: he that is perverse in his ways, shall fall at once.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that tilleth his ground, shall be filled with bread: but he that followeth idleness, shall be filled with poverty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A faithful man shall be much praised: but he that maketh haste to be rich, shall not be innocent.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that hath respect to a person in judgment, doth not well: such a man even for a morsel of bread forsaketh the truth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man that maketh haste to be rich, and envieth others, is ignorant that poverty shall come upon him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that rebuketh a man, shall afterward find favour with him, more than he that by a flattering tongue deceiveth him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that stealeth any thing from his father, or from his mother: and saith, This is no sin, is the partner of a murderer.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that boasteth and puffeth up himself, stirreth up quarrels: but he that trusteth in the Lord, shall be healed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that trusteth in his own heart, is a fool: but he that walketh wisely, he shall be saved.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that giveth to the poor shall not want: he that despiseth his entreaty, shall suffer indigence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When the wicked rise up, men shall hide themselves: when they perish, the just shall be multiplied.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The man that with a stiff neck despiseth him that reproveth him, shall suddenly be destroyed: and health shall not follow him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When just men increase, the people shall rejoice: when the wicked shall bear rule, the people shall mourn.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man that loveth wisdom, rejoiceth his father: but he that maintaineth harlots, shall squander away his substance.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A just king setteth up the land: a covetous man shall destroy it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man that speaketh to his friend with flattering and dissembling words, spreadeth a net for his feet.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A snare shall entangle the wicked man when he sinneth: and the just shall praise and rejoice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The just taketh notice of the cause of the poor: the wicked is void of knowledge.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Corrupt men bring a city to ruin: but wise men turn away wrath.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If a wise man contend with a fool, whether he be angry, or laugh, he shall find no rest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Bloodthirsty men hate the upright: but just men seek his soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A fool uttereth all his mind: a wise man deferreth, and keepeth it till afterwards.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A prince that gladly heareth lying words, hath all his servants wicked.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The poor man and the creditor have met one another: the Lord is the enlightener of them both.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The king that judgeth the poor in truth, his throne shall be established for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The rod and reproof give wisdom: but the child that is left to his own will, bringeth his mother to shame.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When the wicked are multiplied, crimes shall be multiplied: but the just shall see their downfall.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Instruct thy son and he shall refresh thee, and shall give delight to thy soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When prophecy shall fail, the people shall be scattered abroad: but he that keepeth the law, is blessed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A slave will not be corrected by words: because he understandeth what thou sayest, and will not answer.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hast thou seen a man hasty to speak? folly is rather to be looked for, than his amendment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that nourisheth his servant delicately from his childhood, afterwards shall find him stubborn.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A passionate man provoketh quarrels: and he that is easily stirred up to wrath, shall be more prone to sin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Humiliation followeth the proud: and glory shall uphold the humble of spirit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that is partaker with a thief, hateth his own soul: he heareth one putting him to his oath, and discovereth not.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that feareth man shall quickly fall: he that trusteth in the Lord, shall be set on high.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Many seek the face of the prince: but the judgment of every one cometh forth from the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The just abhor a wicked man: and the wicked loathe them that are in the right way. The son that keepeth the word, shall be free from destruction.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The words of Gatherer the son of Vomiter. The vision which the man spoke, with whom God is, and who being strengthened by God, abiding with him, said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I am the most foolish of men, and the wisdom of men is not with me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have not learned wisdom, and have not known the science of saints.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who hath ascended up into heaven, and descended? who hath held the wind in his hands? who hath bound up the waters together as in a garment? who hath raised up all the borders of the earth? what is his name, and what is the name of his son, if thou knowest?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Every word of God is fire tried: he is a buckler to them that hope in him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Add not any thing to his words, lest thou be reproved and found a liar:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Two things I have asked of thee, deny them not to me before I die.
