source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Remove far from me vanity, and lying words. Give me neither beggary, nor riches: give me only the necessaries of life:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lest perhaps being filled, I should be tempted to deny, and say: Who is the Lord? or being compelled by poverty, I should steal, and forswear the name of my God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Accuse not a servant to his master, lest he curse thee, and thou fall.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is a generation that curseth their father, and doth not bless their mother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A generation that are pure in their own eyes, and yet are not washed from their filthiness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A generation, whose eyes are lofty, and their eyelids lifted up on high.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A generation that for teeth hath swords, and grindeth with their jaw teeth, to devour the needy from off the earth, and the poor from among men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The horseleech hath two daughters that say: Bring, bring. There are three things that never are satisfied, and the fourth never saith: It is enough.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hell and the mouth of the womb, and the earth which is not satisfied with water: and the fire never saith: It is enough.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The eye that mocketh at his father, and that despiseth the labour of his mother in bearing him, let the ravens of the brooks pick it out, and the young eagles eat it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Three things are hard to me, and the fourth I am utterly ignorant of.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The way of an eagle in the air, the way of a serpent upon a rock, the way of a ship in the midst of the sea, and the way of a man in youth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Such also is the way of an adulterous woman, who eateth and wipeth her mouth, and saith: I have done no evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By three things the earth is disturbed, and the fourth it cannot bear.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By a slave when he reigneth: by a fool when he is filled with meat:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By an odious woman when she is married: and by a bondwoman when she is heir to her mistress.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There are four very little things of the earth, and they are wiser than the wise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The ants, a feeble people, which provide themselves food in the harvest:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The rabbit, a weak people, which maketh its bed in the rock:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The locust hath no king, yet they all go out by their bands:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The stellio supporteth itself on hands, and dwelleth in kings’ houses.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There are three things, which go well, and the fourth that walketh happily:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A lion, the strongest of beasts, who hath no fear of any thing he meeteth:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A cock girded about the loins: and a ram: and a king, whom none can resist.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is that hath appeared a fool after he was lifted up on high: for if he had understood, he would have laid his hand upon his mouth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he that strongly squeezeth the paps to bring out milk, straineth out butter: and he that violently bloweth his nose, bringeth out blood: and he that provoketh wrath, bringeth forth strife.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The words of king Lamuel. The vision wherewith his mother instructed him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What, O my beloved, what, O the beloved of my womb, what, O the beloved of my vows?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give not thy substance to women, and thy riches to destroy kings.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give not to kings, O Lamuel, give not wine to kings: because there is no secret where drunkenness reigneth:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And lest they drink and forget judgments, and pervert the cause of the children of the poor.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give strong drink to them that are sad; and wine to them that are grieved in mind:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let them drink, and forget their want, and remember their sorrow no more.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Open thy mouth for the dumb, and for the causes of all the children that pass.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Open thy mouth, decree that which is just, and do justice to the needy and poor.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who shall find a valiant woman? far, and from the uttermost coasts is the price of her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The heart of her husband trusteth in her, and he shall have no need of spoils.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She will render him good, and not evil all the days of her life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She hath sought wool and flax, and hath wrought by the counsel of her hands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She is like the merchant’s ship, she bringeth her bread from afar.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she hath risen in the night, and given a prey to her household, and victuals to her maidens.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She hath considered a field, and bought it: with the fruit of her hands she hath planted a vineyard.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She hath girded her loins with strength, and hath strengthened her arm.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She hath tasted, and seen that her traffic is good: her lamp shall not be put out in the night.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She hath put out her hand to strong things, and her fingers have taken hold of the spindle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She hath opened her hand to the needy, and stretched out her hands to the poor.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She shall not fear for her house in the cold of snow: for all her domestics are clothed with double garments.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She hath made for herself clothing of tapestry: fine linen, and purple, is her covering.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Her husband is honourable in the gates, when he sitteth among the senators of the land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She made fine linen, and sold it, and delivered a girdle to the Chanaanite.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Strength and beauty are her clothing, and she shall laugh in the latter day.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She hath opened her mouth to wisdom, and the law of clemency is on her tongue.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She hath looked well on the paths of her house, and hath not eaten her bread idle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Her children rose up, and called her blessed: her husband, and he praised her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Many daughters have gathered together riches: thou hast surpassed them all.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Favour is deceitful, and beauty is vain: the woman that feareth the Lord, she shall be praised.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give her of the fruit of her hands: and let her works praise her in the gates.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner
